summary: DHH analyzes the Epic v Apple verdict, arguing that while Epic didn't achieve everything it wanted, the injunction against Apple's anti-steering provisions effectively kills forced in-app purchases. He lays out a trilemma: Apple's forced IAP regime will either become a joke (as developers openly steer customers to cheaper alternatives), be found illegal (if Apple tries to enforce anti-steering in contempt of court), or simply die as competitive payment processors undercut Apple's 30% tax. He concludes that the practical outcome is a monumental victory for developers even without a complete ruling against Apple's monopoly status.
key_points: The injunction eliminates Apple's anti-steering provisions, allowing developers to openly direct customers to cheaper payment alternatives outside the App Store
Without gag orders, developers can present IAP as an inferior, overpriced option alongside their own payment flows, making Apple's forced IAP a joke
Apple faces a lose-lose: either let developers disparage IAP freely, or try to police it and risk criminal contempt of court
Competitive payment processors like Stripe, Square, and PayPal will undercut Apple's 30% premium once the playing field is leveled
Markets require active intervention from lawmakers, regulators, and judges to prevent capture by dominant players
money_quotes: Apple's forced IAP is either dead, a joke, or illegal.

The rules that say developers can't link to or even tell consumers about other places to buy digital goods and services outside of the App Store, or how much they'd save if they did. That's the so-called anti-steering provisions.
Is Tim Cook going to risk a criminal indictment over an attempt to police language and fixing prices? I doubt it.
With developers free to disparage the thing they don't want to offer but are forced to anyway, it'll give channel to all the pent-up frustrations from years of abusive behavior by Apple.
Apple will simply have to accept competition for developers on a (sorta) level playing field with the likes of Stripe, Square, and PayPal.
Since Adam Smith, it's been broadly recognized that markets can't function like they're supposed to when captured by cartels, duopolies, monopolies, or other dominant market forces.
The market performs its magic when it's kept free of capture. Keeping it so requires eternal vigilance.
key_insight: The Epic v Apple injunction against anti-steering provisions creates an inescapable trilemma that effectively ends Apple's ability to force developers into its overpriced in-app purchase system.
